(6 December 1922 – 29 December 1937), also known by its [Irish](/wiki/Irish-language "Irish-language") name **Saorstát Éireann** (English: [/ˌsɛərstɑːt ˈɛərən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAIR-staht AIR-ən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[4]](#cite_note-8) Irish: [[ˈsˠiːɾˠsˠt̪ˠaːt̪ˠ ˈeːɾʲən̪ˠ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Irish "Help:IPA/Irish")), was the [Irish](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") established in December 1922 under the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") of December 1921. The treaty ended the three-year [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") between the forces of the [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic")—the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1919%E2%80%931922) "Irish Republican Army (1919–1922)") (IRA)—and [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") forces.[[5]](#cite_note-9)

The **Irish Free State** (6 December 1922 – 29 December 1937), also known by its [Irish](/wiki/Irish-language "Irish-language") name **Saorstát Éireann** (English: [/ˌsɛərstɑːt ˈɛərən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAIR-staht AIR-ən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[4]](#cite_note-8) Irish: [[ˈsˠiːɾˠsˠt̪ˠaːt̪ˠ ˈeːɾʲən̪ˠ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Irish "Help:IPA/Irish")), was the [Irish](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") established in December 1922 under the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") of December 1921. The treaty ended the three-year [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") between the forces of the [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic")—the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1919%E2%80%931922) "Irish Republican Army (1919–1922)") (IRA)—and [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") forces.[[5]](#cite_note-9)

The Free State was established as a [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). It comprised 26 of the 32 [counties of Ireland](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland"). [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), which was made up of the remaining six counties, exercised its right under the Treaty to opt out of the new state. The Free State government consisted of the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Governor-General of the Irish Free State")—the representative of the king—and the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Executive Council of the Irish Free State") (cabinet), which replaced both the revolutionary [Dáil Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_2nd_D%C3%A1il "Government of the 2nd Dáil") and the [Provisional Government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_Ireland_(1922) "Provisional Government of Ireland (1922)") set up under the Treaty. [W. T. Cosgrave](/wiki/W._T._Cosgrave "W. T. Cosgrave"), who had led both of these administrations since August 1922, became the first [president of the Executive Council](/wiki/President_of_the_Executive_Council_of_the_Irish_Free_State "President of the Executive Council of the Irish Free State") (prime minister). The [Oireachtas](/wiki/Oireachtas_(Irish_Free_State) "Oireachtas (Irish Free State)") or legislature consisted of [Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Free_State) "Dáil Éireann (Irish Free State)") (the lower house) and [Seanad Éireann](/wiki/Seanad_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Free_State) "Seanad Éireann (Irish Free State)") (the upper house), also known as the Senate. Members of the Dáil were required to take an [Oath of Allegiance to the Constitution of the Free State and to declare fidelity to the king](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Ireland) "Oath of Allegiance (Ireland)"). The oath was a key issue for opponents of the Treaty, who refused to take it and therefore did not take their seats. Pro-Treaty members, who formed [Cumann na nGaedheal](/wiki/Cumann_na_nGaedheal "Cumann na nGaedheal") in 1923, held an effective majority in the Dáil from 1922 to 1927 and thereafter ruled as a minority government until 1932.

In 1931, with the passage of the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") relinquished nearly all of its remaining authority to legislate for the Irish Free State and the other dominions. This granted the Free State internationally recognised independence.

In the first months of the Free State, the [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War") was waged between the newly established [National Army](/wiki/National_Army_(Ireland) "National Army (Ireland)") and the [Anti-Treaty IRA](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1922%E2%80%931969) "Irish Republican Army (1922–1969)"), which refused to recognise the state. The Civil War ended in victory for the government forces, with its opponents dumping their arms in May 1923. The Anti-Treaty political party, [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"), refused to take its seats in the Dáil, leaving the relatively small [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Ireland) "Labour Party (Ireland)") as the only opposition party. In 1926, when Sinn Féin president [Éamon de Valera](/wiki/%C3%89amon_de_Valera "Éamon de Valera") failed to have this policy reversed, he resigned from Sinn Féin and led most of its membership into a new party, [Fianna Fáil](/wiki/Fianna_F%C3%A1il "Fianna Fáil"), which entered the Dáil following the [1927 general election](/wiki/June_1927_Irish_general_election "June 1927 Irish general election"). It formed the government after the [1932 general election](/wiki/1932_Irish_general_election "1932 Irish general election"), when it became the largest party.

De Valera abolished the oath of allegiance and embarked on [an economic war](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_trade_war "Anglo-Irish trade war") with the UK. In 1937, he drafted a new [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland"), which was adopted by a [plebiscite](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Adoption of the Constitution of Ireland") in July of that year. The Free State came to an end with the coming into force of the new constitution on 29 December 1937, when [the state](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") took the name "[Ireland](/wiki/Names_of_the_Irish_state "Names of the Irish state")".

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") of 1916 and its aftermath caused a profound shift in public opinion towards [the republican cause in Ireland](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism").[[6]](#cite_note-10) In the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland"), the republican [Sinn Féin](/wiki/History_of_Sinn_F%C3%A9in "History of Sinn Féin") party won a large majority of the Irish seats in the British parliament: 73 of the 105 constituencies returned Sinn Féin members (25 uncontested).[[7]](#cite_note-11) The elected Sinn Féin MPs, rather than take their seats at Westminster, set up their own assembly, known as [Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Republic) "Dáil Éireann (Irish Republic)") (Assembly of Ireland). It affirmed the formation of an Irish Republic and passed a [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Irish_Declaration_of_Independence "Irish Declaration of Independence").[[8]](#cite_note-12) The subsequent [War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"), fought between the Irish Republican Army (IRA) and British security forces, continued until July 1921 when a truce came into force. By this time the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland") had opened, established under the [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920"), presenting the republican movement with a *fait accompli* and guaranteeing the British presence in Ireland.[[9]](#cite_note-13) In October negotiations opened in London between members of the British government and members of the Dáil, culminating in the signing of the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") on 6 December 1921.[[10]](#cite_note-Lee50-14)

The Treaty allowed for the creation of a separate state to be known as the Irish Free State, with dominion status, within the British Empire—a status equivalent to Canada.[[10]](#cite_note-Lee50-14) The Parliament of Northern Ireland could, by presenting an address to the king, opt not to be included in the Free State, in which case a [Boundary Commission](/wiki/Irish_Boundary_Commission "Irish Boundary Commission") would be established to determine where the boundary between them should lie.[[11]](#cite_note-15)[[12]](#cite_note-16) Members of the parliament of the Free State would be required to take an [oath of allegiance to the Constitution of the Free State](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Ireland) "Oath of Allegiance (Ireland)") and to declare that they would be "faithful" to the king (a modification of the oath taken in other dominions).[[10]](#cite_note-Lee50-14)

The [Dáil](/wiki/Second_D%C3%A1il "Second Dáil") ratified the Treaty on 7 January 1922, causing a split in the republican movement.[[13]](#cite_note-17) A [Provisional Government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_Ireland_(1922) "Provisional Government of Ireland (1922)") was formed, with [Michael Collins](/wiki/Michael_Collins_(Irish_leader) "Michael Collins (Irish leader)") as chairman.[[14]](#cite_note-18)

The Free State was established on 6 December 1922, and the Provisional Government became the [Executive Council of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Executive Council of the Irish Free State"), headed by [W. T. Cosgrave](/wiki/W._T._Cosgrave "W. T. Cosgrave") as [President of the Executive Council](/wiki/President_of_the_Executive_Council_of_the_Irish_Free_State "President of the Executive Council of the Irish Free State").[[15]](#cite_note-19) The following day, the Commons and the Senate of Northern Ireland passed resolutions "for the express purpose of opting out of the Free State".[[16]](#cite_note-20)[[e]](#cite_note-23)

## Governmental and constitutional structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governmental and constitutional structures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/23/Saorst%C3%A1t_%C3%89ireann.png/250px-Saorst%C3%A1t_%C3%89ireann.png)](/wiki/File:Saorst%C3%A1t_%C3%89ireann.png)

A symbol most often associated with the new state's [postal system](/wiki/Minister_for_Posts_and_Telegraphs "Minister for Posts and Telegraphs")

The Treaty established that the new state would be a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), with the [Governor-General of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Governor-General_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Governor-General of the Irish Free State") as the representative of the Crown. The *[Constitution of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Constitution of the Irish Free State")* made more detailed provision for the state's system of government, with a three-tier parliament, called the [Oireachtas](/wiki/Oireachtas_(Irish_Free_State) "Oireachtas (Irish Free State)"), made up of the king and two houses, Dáil Éireann and [Seanad Éireann](/wiki/Seanad_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Free_State) "Seanad Éireann (Irish Free State)") (the Irish Senate).

Executive authority was vested in the king, with the Governor-General as his representative. He appointed a cabinet called the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Executive Council of the Irish Free State") to "aid and advise" him. The Executive Council was presided over by a prime minister called the [President of the Executive Council](/wiki/President_of_the_Executive_Council_of_the_Irish_Free_State "President of the Executive Council of the Irish Free State"). In practice, most of the real power was exercised by the Executive Council, as the Governor-General was almost always bound to act on the advice of the Executive Council.

### Representative of the Crown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Representative of the Crown")]

Main article: [Governor-General of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Governor-General_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Governor-General of the Irish Free State")

The office of Governor-General of the Irish Free State replaced the previous [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Ireland "Lord Lieutenant of Ireland"), who had headed English and British administrations in Ireland since the Middle Ages. Governors-General were appointed by the king initially on the advice of the British Government, but with the consent of the Irish Government. From 1927, the Irish Government alone had the power to advise the king whom to appoint.

### Oath of Allegiance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Oath of Allegiance")]

As with all dominions, provision was made for an [Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance "Oath of Allegiance"). Within dominions, such oaths were taken by parliamentarians personally towards the monarch. The [Irish Oath of Allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_Allegiance_(Ireland) "Oath of Allegiance (Ireland)") was fundamentally different. It had two elements; the first, an *oath to the Free State, as by law established*, the second part a promise of *fidelity, to His Majesty, King George V, his heirs and successors*. That second fidelity element, however, was qualified in two ways. It was to the king *in* Ireland, not specifically to the king of the United Kingdom. Secondly, it was to the king explicitly in his role as part of the Treaty settlement, not in terms of pre-1922 British rule. The Oath itself came from a combination of three sources, and was largely the work of Michael Collins in the Treaty negotiations. It came in part from a draft oath suggested prior to the negotiations by President de Valera. Other sections were taken by Collins directly from the Oath of the [Irish Republican Brotherhood](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Brotherhood "Irish Republican Brotherhood") (IRB), of which he was the secret head. In its structure, it was also partially based on the form and structure used for 'Dominion status'.[[19]](#cite_note-24)

Although 'a new departure', and notably indirect in its reference to the monarchy, it was criticised by nationalists and republicans for making any reference to the Crown, the claim being that it *was* a direct oath to the Crown, a fact arguably incorrect by an examination of its wording, but in 1922 Ireland and beyond, many argued that the fact remained that as a dominion the king (and therefore the British) was still [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and that was the practical reality that influenced public debate on the issue. The Free State was not a republic. The Oath became a key issue in the resulting [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War") that divided the pro and anti-treaty sides in 1922–23.

## Irish Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Irish Civil War")]

Main article: [Irish Civil War](/wiki/Irish_Civil_War "Irish Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Crowds_at_funeral_procession_of_Michael_Collins_Dublin_1922_%286088554764%29.jpg/250px-Crowds_at_funeral_procession_of_Michael_Collins_Dublin_1922_%286088554764%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowds_at_funeral_procession_of_Michael_Collins_Dublin_1922_(6088554764).jpg)

Funeral procession of Michael Collins, Dublin, 1922

The compromises contained in the agreement caused the civil war in the 26 counties in June 1922 – April 1923, in which the pro-Treaty [Provisional Government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_Ireland_(1922) "Provisional Government of Ireland (1922)") defeated the anti-Treaty Republican forces. The latter were led, nominally, by [Éamon de Valera](/wiki/%C3%89amon_de_Valera "Éamon de Valera"), who had resigned as President of the Republic on the treaty's ratification. His resignation outraged some of his own supporters, notably [Seán T. O'Kelly](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_T._O%27Kelly "Seán T. O'Kelly"), the main Sinn Féin organiser. On resigning, he then sought re-election but was defeated two days later on a vote of 60–58. The pro-Treaty Arthur Griffith followed as President of the Irish Republic. Michael Collins was chosen at a meeting of the members elected to sit in the [House of Commons of Southern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Southern_Ireland "Parliament of Southern Ireland") (a body set up under the Government of Ireland Act 1920) to become [Chairman of the Provisional Government of the Irish Free State](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Provisional_Government_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Chairman of the Provisional Government of the Irish Free State") in accordance with the Treaty. The [general election](/wiki/1922_Irish_general_election "1922 Irish general election") in June gave overwhelming support for the pro-Treaty parties. [W. T. Cosgrave](/wiki/W._T._Cosgrave "W. T. Cosgrave")'s Crown-appointed Provisional Government effectively subsumed Griffith's republican administration with the death of both Collins and Griffith in August 1922.[[20]](#cite_note-25)

The day after the founding of the Irish Free State, anti-Treaty IRA men shot and killed [Seán Hales](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_Hales "Seán Hales") an elected member of the lower house of Parliament ([Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann "Dáil Éireann")). The next day (8 December 1922), four imprisoned leaders of the anti-Treaty IRA ([Liam Mellows](/wiki/Liam_Mellows "Liam Mellows"), [Rory O'Connor](/wiki/Rory_O%27Connor_(Irish_republican) "Rory O'Connor (Irish republican)"), [Joe McKelvey](/wiki/Joe_McKelvey "Joe McKelvey") and [Dick Barrett](/wiki/Richard_Barrett_(Irish_republican) "Richard Barrett (Irish republican)")) were executed in retaliation for the killing of Hales.[[21]](#cite_note-26)

## "Freedom to achieve freedom"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: \"Freedom to achieve freedom\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Irish_Free_State_passport.jpg/250px-Irish_Free_State_passport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irish_Free_State_passport.jpg)

[Irish Free State passport](/wiki/Irish_passport "Irish passport") (holder's name removed)

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Governance")]

The following were the principal parties of government of the Free State between 1922 and 1937:

* [Cumann na nGaedheal](/wiki/Cumann_na_nGaedheal "Cumann na nGaedheal") under W. T. Cosgrave (1922–32)
* [Fianna Fáil](/wiki/Fianna_F%C3%A1il "Fianna Fáil") under Éamon de Valera (1932–37)

### Constitutional evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Constitutional evolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Stamp_irl_1922_2N6se.jpg/250px-Stamp_irl_1922_2N6se.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_irl_1922_2N6se.jpg)

[Overprinted](/wiki/Overprint "Overprint") stamp

Michael Collins described the Treaty as "the freedom to achieve freedom". In practice, the Treaty offered most of the symbols and powers of independence. These included a functioning, if disputed, [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") with its own executive, judiciary and written constitution which could be changed by the Oireachtas. Although an [Irish republic](/wiki/Irish_republic "Irish republic") had not been on offer, the Treaty still afforded Ireland more internal independence than it had possessed in over 400 years, and far more autonomy than had ever been hoped for by those who had advocated for [Home Rule](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement").[[22]](#cite_note-27)

However, a number of conditions existed:

* The king remained king *in* Ireland;
* Britain retained the so-called strategic [Treaty Ports](/wiki/Treaty_Ports_(Ireland) "Treaty Ports (Ireland)") on Ireland's south and north-west coasts which were to remain occupied by the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy");
* Prior to the passage of the [Statute of Westminster](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931"), the UK government continued to have a role in Irish governance. Officially the representative of the king, the Governor-General also received instructions from the British Government on his use of the [Royal Assent](/wiki/Royal_assent_(Ireland) "Royal assent (Ireland)"), namely a Bill passed by the Dáil and Seanad could be Granted Assent (signed into law), Withheld (not signed, pending later approval) or Denied (vetoed). The [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") to the first Governor-General, [Tim Healy](/wiki/Tim_Healy_(politician) "Tim Healy (politician)"), explicitly named Bills that were to be rejected if passed by the Dáil and Seanad, such as any attempt to abolish the Oath. No such Bills were ever introduced.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Irish_Free_State_Butter%2C_Eggs_and_Bacon_for_our_Breakfasts_Du_beurre%2C_des_%C5%93ufs_et_du_bacon_de_l%E2%80%99%C3%89tat_libre_d%E2%80%99Irlande_au_d%C3%A9jeuner.jpg/250px-Irish_Free_State_Butter%2C_Eggs_and_Bacon_for_our_Breakfasts_Du_beurre%2C_des_%C5%93ufs_et_du_bacon_de_l%E2%80%99%C3%89tat_libre_d%E2%80%99Irlande_au_d%C3%A9jeuner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irish_Free_State_Butter,_Eggs_and_Bacon_for_our_Breakfasts_Du_beurre,_des_%C5%93ufs_et_du_bacon_de_l%E2%80%99%C3%89tat_libre_d%E2%80%99Irlande_au_d%C3%A9jeuner.jpg)

Poster promoting Irish Free State farm goods for breakfast to Canadians ("Irish Free State butter, eggs and bacon for our breakfasts")

* As with the other dominions, the Free State had a status of association with the UK rather than being completely legally independent from it. However, the meaning of 'Dominion status' changed radically during the 1920s, starting with the [Chanak crisis](/wiki/Chanak_Crisis "Chanak Crisis") in 1922 and quickly followed by the directly negotiated [Halibut Treaty](/wiki/Halibut_Treaty "Halibut Treaty") of 1923. The [1926 Imperial Conference](/wiki/1926_Imperial_Conference "1926 Imperial Conference") declared the equality [including the UK] of all member states of the Commonwealth. The Conference also led to a reform of the king's title, given effect by the *[Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927")*, which changed the king's royal title so that it took account of the fact that there was no longer a [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). The king adopted the following style by which he would be known in all of his empire: *By the Grace of God, of Great Britain, Ireland and the British Dominions beyond the Seas King, Defender of the Faith, Emperor of India*. That was the king's title in Ireland just as elsewhere in the British Empire.[[23]](#cite_note-28)
* In the conduct of external relations, the Free State tried to push the boundaries of its status as a Dominion. It 'accepted' credentials from international ambassadors to Ireland, something no other dominion up to then had done. It registered the treaty with the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") as an international document, over the objections of the United Kingdom, which saw it as a mere *internal* document between a dominion and the United Kingdom. Entitlement of citizenship of the Free State was defined in the Free State Constitution, but the status of that citizenship was contentious. One of the first projects of the Free State was the design and production of the [Great Seal of Saorstát Éireann](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_Irish_Free_State "Great Seal of the Irish Free State") which was carried out on behalf of the Government by [Hugh Kennedy](/wiki/Hugh_Kennedy_(judge) "Hugh Kennedy (judge)").

The Statute of Westminster of 1931, embodying a decision of an Imperial Conference, enabled each dominion to enact new legislation and change any extant legislation, without resorting to the U.K. Parliament (or any Crown intermediary). Extant legislation includes any and all Dominion legislation enacted prior to the Statute of Westminster. It also removed Westminster's authority to legislate for the Dominions, except by the express request and consent of the relevant Dominion's parliament. This change made the dominions, including the Free State, *de jure* independent nations—thus fulfilling Collins' vision of having "the freedom to achieve freedom".

The Free State symbolically marked these changes in two mould-breaking moves soon after winning internationally recognised independence:

* It sought, and got, the king's acceptance to have an Irish minister, to the complete exclusion of British ministers, formally advise the king in the exercise of his powers and functions as king in the Free State. This gave the President of the Executive Council the right to directly advise the king in his capacity as His Majesty's Irish Prime Minister. Two examples of this are the signing of a treaty between the Free State and the [Portuguese Republic](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") in 1931, and the act recognising the [abdication](/wiki/Abdication_of_Edward_VIII "Abdication of Edward VIII") of [King Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") in 1936 separately from the recognition by the British Parliament.
* The unprecedented replacement of the use of the [Great Seal of the Realm](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_Realm "Great Seal of the Realm") and its replacement by the Great Seal of Saorstát Éireann, which the king awarded to the Free State in 1931. (The Irish Seal consisted of a picture of King George V enthroned on one side, with the Irish state harp and the words *Saorstát Éireann* on the reverse. It is now on display in the Irish National Museum, [Collins Barracks, Dublin](/wiki/Collins_Barracks,_Dublin "Collins Barracks, Dublin").)

When Éamon de Valera became President of the Executive Council (prime minister) in 1932 he described Cosgrave's ministers' achievements simply. Having read the files, he told his son, Vivion, "they were magnificent, son".

The Statute of Westminster allowed de Valera, on becoming President of the Executive Council (February 1932), to go even further. With no ensuing restrictions on his policies, he abolished the Oath of Allegiance (which Cosgrave intended to do had he won the [1932 general election](/wiki/1932_Irish_general_election "1932 Irish general election")), the [Seanad](/wiki/Seanad_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Free_State) "Seanad Éireann (Irish Free State)"), university representation in the Dáil, and appeals to the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council").

One major policy error occurred in 1936 when he attempted to use the [abdication of King Edward VIII](/wiki/Abdication_of_King_Edward_VIII "Abdication of King Edward VIII") to abolish the crown and governor-general in the Free State with the "[Constitution (Amendment No. 27) Act](/wiki/Constitution_(Amendment_No._27)_Act_1936 "Constitution (Amendment No. 27) Act 1936")". He was advised by senior law officers and other constitutional experts that, as the crown and governor-generalship existed separately from the constitution in a vast number of acts, charters, orders-in-council, and letters patent, they both still existed. A second bill, the "[Executive Powers (Consequential Provisions) Act, 1937](/wiki/Executive_Powers_(Consequential_Provisions)_Act_1937 "Executive Powers (Consequential Provisions) Act 1937")" was quickly introduced to repeal the necessary elements. De Valera retroactively dated the second act back to December 1936.

### Currency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Currency")]

The new state continued to use the [Pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling") from its inception; there is no reference in the Treaty or in either of the enabling Acts to currency.[[24]](#cite_note-29) Nonetheless, and within a few years, the Dáil passed the Coinage Act, 1926 (which provided for a *Saorstát* [Free State] coinage) and the Currency Act, 1927 (which provided *inter alia* for banknotes of the [Saorstát pound](/wiki/Irish_Pound#Second_pound "Irish Pound")). The new Saorstát pound was defined by the 1927 Act to have exactly the same weight and fineness of gold as was the [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign_(British_coin) "Sovereign (British coin)") at the time, making the new currency [pegged](/wiki/Fixed_exchange_rate_system "Fixed exchange rate system") at 1:1 with sterling. The State circulated its new national coinage in December 1928, marked *Saorstát Éireann* and a [national series of banknotes](/wiki/Series_A_banknotes "Series A banknotes"). British coinage remained acceptable in the Free State at an equal rate. In 1937, when the Free State was superseded by Ireland (*Éire*), the pound became known as the "[Irish pound](/wiki/Irish_pound "Irish pound")" and the coins were marked *Éire* as from 1939.

No coins dated 1938 were struck for circulation in Ireland, but the 1938 1 Penny and Half Crown exists as [pattern coins](/wiki/Pattern_coin "Pattern coin").

## Foreign policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Foreign policy")]

Ireland joined the League of Nations on 10 September 1923.[[25]](#cite_note-30) It would also participate in the Olympics sending its first team to the 1924 Summer Olympics held in Paris.[[26]](#cite_note-31) They would send further teams to the [1928 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics") and the [1932 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics").[[27]](#cite_note-32)

According to Gerard Keown, by 1932 much had been achieved in the quest for an independent foreign policy.[[28]](#cite_note-33)

> The Free State was an established element in the European system and a member of the League of Nations. It had blazed a trail in asserting the rights of the dominions to their own foreign policy, in the process establishing full diplomatic relations with the United States, France, Belgium, Germany, and the Holy See. It was concluding its own political and commercial treaties and using the apparatus of international relations to pursue its interests. It had received the accolade of election to a non-permanent seat on the council of the League of Nations and asserted its full equality with Britain and the other dominions within the Commonwealth.

By contrast, the military was drastically reduced in size and scope, with its budget cut by 82% from 1924 to 1929. The active duty forces were reduced from 28,000 men to 7,000. Co-operation with London was minimal.[[29]](#cite_note-34)[[30]](#cite_note-35)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Demographics")]

### Birth rate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Birth rate")]

According to one report, in 1924, shortly after the Free State's establishment, the new dominion had the "lowest birth-rate in the world". The report noted that amongst countries for which statistics were available ([Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon"), Chile, [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), [Spain](/wiki/Restoration_(Spain) "Restoration (Spain)"), [South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa"), the Netherlands, Canada, [Germany](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), Australia, the United States, [Britain](/wiki/Interwar_Britain "Interwar Britain"), [New Zealand](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_Zealand "Dominion of New Zealand"), Finland, and the Irish Free State), Ceylon had the highest birth rate at 40.8 per 1,000 while the Irish Free State had a birth rate of just 18.6 per 1,000.[[31]](#cite_note-36)

### Cultural outlook

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cultural outlook")]

Irish society during this period was extremely Roman Catholic,[[32]](#cite_note-37) with Roman Catholic thinkers promoting anti-capitalist, anti-communist, anti-Protestant, anti-Masonic, and antisemitic views in Irish society.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Through the works of priests such as [Edward Cahill](/wiki/Edward_Cahill_(priest) "Edward Cahill (priest)"), [Richard Devane](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Devane&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard Devane (page does not exist)"), and [Denis Fahey](/wiki/Denis_Fahey "Denis Fahey"), Irish society saw capitalism, individualism, communism, private banking, the promotion of alcohol, contraceptives, divorce, and abortion as the pursuits of the old 'Protestant-elite' and Jews, with their efforts combined through the [Freemasons](/wiki/Freemasonry "Freemasonry"). Denis Fahey described Ireland as "the third most Masonic country in the world" and saw this alleged order as contrary to the creation of an independent Irish State.[[33]](#cite_note-38)

## After the Free State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: After the Free State")]

### 1937 Constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1937 Constitution")]

In 1937, the Fianna Fáil government presented a draft of an entirely new Constitution to Dáil Éireann. An amended version of the draft document was subsequently approved by the Dáil. A [plebiscite](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Adoption of the Constitution of Ireland") was held on 1 July 1937, which was the same day as the [1937 general election](/wiki/1937_Irish_general_election "1937 Irish general election"), when a relatively narrow majority approved it. The new [Constitution of Ireland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland") (*Bunreacht na hÉireann*) repealed the 1922 Constitution, and came into effect on 29 December 1937.[[34]](#cite_note-39)

The state was named Ireland ([Éire](/wiki/%C3%89ire "Éire") in the [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language")), and a new office of [President of Ireland](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland") was instituted in place of the Governor-General of the Irish Free State. The new constitution claimed jurisdiction over all of Ireland while recognising that legislation would not apply in Northern Ireland (see [Articles 2 and 3](/wiki/Articles_2_and_3_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Articles 2 and 3 of the Constitution of Ireland")). Articles 2 and 3 were [reworded](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Nineteenth Amendment of the Constitution of Ireland") in 1998 to remove jurisdictional claim over the entire island and to recognise that "a united Ireland shall be brought about only by peaceful means with the consent of a majority of the people, democratically expressed, in both jurisdictions in the island".

With regard to religion, a section of Article 44 included the following:

> The State recognises the special position of the Holy Catholic Apostolic and Roman Church as the guardian of the Faith professed by the great majority of the citizens. The State also recognises the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"), the [Presbyterian Church in Ireland](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_Ireland "Presbyterian Church in Ireland"), the [Methodist Church in Ireland](/wiki/Methodist_Church_in_Ireland "Methodist Church in Ireland"), the [Religious Society of Friends in Ireland](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends_in_Ireland "Religious Society of Friends in Ireland"), as well as the [Jewish Congregations](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Ireland "History of the Jews in Ireland") and the other religious denominations existing in Ireland at the date of the coming into operation of this Constitution.

Following a referendum, this section was [removed](/wiki/Fifth_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_Ireland "Fifth Amendment of the Constitution of Ireland") in 1973. After the setting up of the Free State in 1923, unionism in the south largely came to an end.

The 1937 Constitution saw a notable ideological slant to the changes in the framework of the State in such a way as to create one that appeared to be distinctly Irish. This was done by implementing [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatism "Corporatism") policies (based on the concepts of the Roman [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), as Catholicism was perceived to be deeply embedded with the perception of Irish identity). A clear example of this is the model of the reconstituted [Seanad Éireann](/wiki/Seanad_%C3%89ireann "Seanad Éireann") (the Senate), which operates based on a system of vocational panels, along with a list of appointed nominating industry bodies, a corporatist concept (seen in [Pope Pius XI's](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") 1931 encyclical [Quadragesimo anno](/wiki/Quadragesimo_anno "Quadragesimo anno")). Furthermore, Ireland's main political parties, Fine Gael, Fianna Fáil and Labour, all had an inherently corporatist outlook.[[35]](#cite_note-40)[[36]](#cite_note-41)[[37]](#cite_note-42)[[38]](#cite_note-43)[[39]](#cite_note-44)[[40]](#cite_note-45) The government was the subject of intense lobbying by leading Church figures throughout the 1930s in calling for reform of the State's framework. Much of this was reflected in the new 1937 Constitution.[[41]](#cite_note-46)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Free_State&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the Irish Free State considered each other states with equal status within the British Empire. However, for other sovereign states (the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic"), [Brazil](/wiki/First_Brazilian_Republic "First Brazilian Republic"), [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire"), etc) and the international community as a whole (i.e [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")), the term Dominion was very ambiguous. At the time of the founding of the League of Nations in 1924, the League Covenant made provision for the admission of any "fully self-governing state, Dominion, or Colony", the implication being that "Dominion status was something between that of a colony and a state"
2. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** With the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") the ambiguity is dispelled for the international community with three British Dominions (Irish Free State, [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa")), being recognized as sovereign states in their own right. Unlike what happened in Australia in 1942 and New Zealand in 1947, the whole statute was applied to the Dominion of Canada, the Irish Free State, and the Union of South Africa without the need for any acts of ratification.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** [National language](/wiki/National_language "National language")
4. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Co-official and the most widely spoken language
5. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** On a strict interpretation, the Treaty, and the [Irish Free State (Agreement) Act 1922](/wiki/Irish_Free_State_(Agreement)_Act_1922 "Irish Free State (Agreement) Act 1922") which gave it force of law, had the legal effect of making Northern Ireland a part of the Free State for one or two days. This would later have an effect in nationality law, an Irish court ruling in 1933 that the citizens of Northern Ireland had become Irish citizens as well in 1922.[[17]](#cite_note-Legal_Analysis-21) The *de facto* position was that Northern Ireland was treated as at all times being within the United Kingdom. The terms of the Treaty applied only to the 26 counties, and the government of the Free State never had any powers—even in principle—in Northern Ireland.[[18]](#cite_note-22)
